description: Aparajita means the one who cannot be defeated, and that single idea runs through these twelve verses praising Goddess Durga. The hymn comes from the Markandeya Purana, where the Goddess is addressed in her invincible form. Devotees turn to it for victory in whatever they undertake and for protection against defeat; the older tradition also reaches for it to clear away fear, sin, and inauspiciousness, and to draw down wisdom, prosperity, and divine grace. Tuesdays and Fridays are the usual recitation days, with Navratri and Vijayadashami marking the high points of the year. A full reading runs about seven minutes, short enough to fit a daily routine. Below you will find the complete text in Devanagari, a Roman transliteration to help with pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline use.

Few texts state non-duality as bluntly as the Avadhuta Gita, the song of Dattatreya. This selection gathers fifteen of its key verses, the ones that carry the highest Advaita teaching: that Atman and Brahman are one, and that recognizing this is liberation. Read them and the mind loosens its grip on bondage, attachment, and the sense of two-ness; what rises in their place is vairagya, dispassion, and jnana, the transcendental knowledge the avadhuta embodies. Thursday is the day most associated with the recitation, and Guru Purnima and Datta Jayanti are when it carries special weight. Plan on roughly ten minutes for the full passage. The page presents every verse in Devanagari alongside a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a downloadable PDF you can keep offline.

What kind of faith do you actually hold? Chapter 17 of the Bhagavad Gita, the Shraddhatraya Vibhaga Yoga from the Mahabharata, takes up that question across twenty-eight verses. Krishna explains how shraddha shapes a person's nature and destiny, sorts faith into its sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic grades, and ties each to the food one eats, the austerities one keeps, and the gifts one gives. The chapter closes on the three syllables Om Tat Sat as the threefold designation of Brahman. Threefold austerity, of body, speech, and mind, is laid out plainly here. Wednesdays are favored for recitation, and Janmashtami above all. A careful reading takes about fourteen minutes. You can read the whole chapter in Devanagari with Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline study.

Drawn from the Rudra Yamala Tantra, the Bhairava Hridayam is the heart-hymn of Lord Bhairava, eight tightly wound verses meant to summon his supreme protection. Tantric practitioners recite it to be rid of fears, diseases, and sorrows, and to invoke Bhairava as Kshetrapala, the guardian of place and ground. Victory and freedom from every affliction are the blessings sought through it. The hymn keeps the company of Saturdays and Tuesdays, and Kala Bhairava Ashtami is its festival. Being so brief, eight verses, it takes only about four minutes to complete, which makes daily recitation easy to sustain. Here the full text appears in Devanagari, followed by a Roman transliteration for those who do not read the script, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF you can download and recite offline.

A Sahasranama for Brahma is a genuine rarity. Most thousand-name hymns belong to Vishnu, Shiva, or the Goddess, which makes this composition from the Brahma Purana an unusual find in praise of the Creator himself. Across its twelve verses it names Brahma in every aspect, as Prajapati, as Virinchi, as the source of creation, and reciting it is held to bestow supreme creative power along with the knowledge of all the Vedas. Thursday is the recommended day; Brahma Jayanti and Guru Purnima are its festivals. The recitation runs to about eight minutes. On this page the complete stotram is given in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Before worship comes the seeing. The Dakshina Kali Dhyanam, fourteen verses drawn from the Todala Tantra and Kali Tantra, fixes in the mind the iconic four-armed, south-facing form of Kali, the benevolent one who grants abhaya and vara, fearlessness and boon, with raised hands. Tradition holds that meditation on this form alone, sarva-kamartha-siddhi, fulfills every desire, and bestows mastery over all the siddhis and the three worlds. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days; Kali Puja, Diwali, and Navratri its seasons. About nine minutes carries you through the full dhyana. The verses are set out here in Devanagari, paired with a Roman transliteration for ease of chanting, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF you can save and recite offline.

Dattatreya joins Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva in one body, the Trimurti as a single guru, and this eight-verse Hridayam from the Dattatreya Tantra is his heart-hymn. Reciters invoke through it that combined power of the three, along with the guru's grace, knowledge, and the liberation it leads to; the classical line also turns to it to remove diseases and calamities. Thursday, the guru's own day, is when it is most often read, and Datta Jayanti and Guru Purnima are its festivals. At eight verses it finishes in roughly four minutes, equally suited to a quiet daily practice or a festival observance. The complete text waits below in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Of all the hymns to the Divine Mother, this Devi Stuti from the Devi Mahatmyam in the Markandeya Purana may be the most loved and most widely recited. Its twenty-five verses see the Goddess in every cosmic manifestation and within every being, and the act of reciting them awakens both devotion and the recognition of her presence throughout creation. What devotees ask of it is wisdom, peace, strength, and divine grace. Tuesdays and Fridays are the customary days, and Navratri is its festival. The reading takes around ten minutes, comfortable for a daily routine. You will find the full stuti here in Devanagari, accompanied by a Roman transliteration for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download to keep for offline recitation.

Tulsidas wrote far more than the Ramcharitmanas, and his Dohavali holds some of his sharpest thinking in couplet form. This selection gathers fourteen of its key dohe, a working treasury of his wisdom on devotion, ethics, and detachment. The Rama Nama couplets keep the name of the divine constantly in mind; the niti dohe offer plain, usable guidance for living rightly; and the Sant Mahima verses press the value of holy company. Sunday is the day tied to the recitation, with Ram Navami as its festival. Reading through the selection takes about ten minutes. Each couplet appears here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to follow along, the Hindi meaning (arth) spelled out, and a free PDF you can download and read offline.

On the eve of the Mahabharata war, at Krishna's instruction, Arjuna stepped down from his chariot and prayed to Durga, and that prayer is preserved in the Bhishma Parva as thirteen verses of fierce praise. Because Krishna himself commanded it, the stuti carries his endorsement, and it brought Arjuna the victory he sought. Reciters invoke it for victory in battles, competitions, and every kind of confrontation, calling on Durga in her warrior forms for supreme martial protection; tradition promises that nothing in the three worlds stays out of reach for one who recites it. Tuesdays and Fridays suit it, with Navratri and Vijayadashami as its festivals. The reading runs about eight minutes. The full text is here in Devanagari with Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline use.

The Ganesh Atharvashirsha carries Vedic weight that most later hymns do not, drawn as it is from the Atharvaveda itself. Its twenty-eight verses praise Ganesha as the remover of obstacles and hindrances, and devotees recite them seeking exactly that, along with intellectual clarity, success across every undertaking, and the spiritual enlightenment the text points toward. Wednesday is its day, and Ganesh Chaturthi its great festival. A full recitation takes around twelve minutes, slow enough to honor the Vedic cadence. The complete Atharvashirsha is laid out on this page in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration to help with the demanding pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each section, and a free PDF download so you can recite it offline whenever you wish.

A kavacham is armor, and the Ganga Kavacham wraps the reciter in the protective power of Mother Ganga herself. These eight verses from the Skanda Purana invoke her by sixteen sacred names, Jahnavi, Bhagirathi, Mandakini and the rest, calling on each to guard a direction so that protection comes from every side. Purification is the other gift here: the river's grace is held to wash away sins and negative karma. Monday is the day for it, and Ganga Dussehra the festival on which it carries most force. Eight short verses make for a four-minute recitation. The full kavacham is presented in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) given in full, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

When Krishna lifted Mount Govardhan to shelter the cowherds from Indra's storm, he earned the name Giridhari, the lifter of the hill, and these eight verses from the tenth canto of the Bhagavata Purana praise exactly that form. The lila they invoke teaches complete surrender to Krishna as the supreme protector, and in the telling Indra's pride is broken as the devotee's ego is meant to be. The stotram clears obstacles and calamities through Krishna's grace and grants fearlessness and his protection. Wednesday is its day; Janmashtami and Govardhan Puja its festivals, making it a natural fit for the Annakut celebration. About five minutes covers the recitation. The full text appears here in Devanagari with Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline reading.

When Krishna vanished from the rasa dance, the gopis sang, and their song is recorded as the Gopika Gitam in the tenth canto of the Srimad Bhagavatam, at chapter thirty-one. Nine verses of longing, it holds the celebrated line Tava kathamritam on the nectar of hearing about Hari, words that have moved devotees for centuries. Reciting the gita awakens prema, divine love, in the heart and deepens one's grasp of the gopis' supreme devotion. Wednesdays and Fridays are the days for it, with Janmashtami and Sharad Purnima, the night of the rasa dance, as its festivals. The reading takes about ten minutes. You will find all nine verses here in Devanagari, a Roman transliteration set beside them, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Brihaspati, the guru among the planets, governs wisdom, wealth, and the growth of a family, and this eight-verse stotra from the Skanda Purana is addressed to him. Where Jupiter sits afflicted in a horoscope, the classical remedy is recitation, which is held to clear those afflictions and draw down knowledge, spiritual enlightenment, and material prosperity, wealth, grains, and the blessing of children and grandchildren. Thursday, Guru's own day, is when it is recited. There is no fixed festival attached to it; it belongs to weekly planetary practice. Eight verses come to about five minutes. The complete stotra is given on this page in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to ease pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF you can download and recite offline.

The Lanka Kanda of Valmiki's Ramayana is where Hanuman's strength shows at full stretch, and this stuti of eight verses draws its praise straight from that book, recounting his heroic exploits during the war. The verses are meant to kindle courage and resolve in those who recite them and to plant fearlessness in the face of hardship; the older tradition also reaches for them to drive off diseases and obstacles. Tuesdays and Saturdays are the days associated with the stuti, and Hanuman Jayanti is its festival. A full reading takes roughly seven minutes. The complete text sits here in Devanagari, paired with a Roman transliteration for those who chant by sound, the Hindi meaning (arth) given plainly, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Adi Shankaracharya composed this Ashtakam in eight verses, each closing with a plea that the Lord of the Universe at Puri cast his glance upon the devotee. It is a hymn for devotion and spiritual growth, invoking Jagannath in all his forms and asking for his protection and auspiciousness; tradition also recites it to wipe away sin and earn spiritual merit. Thursday is its day, and Rath Yatra and Janmashtami its festivals, the seasons when Jagannath worship reaches its height. The eight verses take about five minutes to recite. On this page the complete Ashtakam is set out in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration running alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download you can keep for offline recitation.

When Rahu and Ketu hem in every other planet, astrologers call it Kaal Sarp Dosha, and this stotram of eleven verses, drawn from traditional Jyotish Shastra and addressed to Lord Shiva, is its primary remedy. Recitation is meant to neutralize the malefic pull of those shadow planets, calm the fear of serpents and the snake-haunted dreams that often accompany the dosha, and settle the mind into peace and spiritual stability. Monday is the day for it, with Nag Panchami and Maha Shivaratri as its festivals. The reading runs about seven minutes. The full stotram appears here in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning (arth) laid out, and a free PDF download so you can recite it offline.

Marriage, love, and a happy married life are what people bring to the Kamadeva Stotram, a ten-verse hymn from Puranic compilation associated with Lord Vishnu. It carries the Kamadeva Gayatri for invoking the deity of love directly, and reciting it is held to lend the devotee beauty, charm, and attractiveness while bestowing saubhagya, marital bliss and good fortune. Friday is its day, and Holi, the spring festival long tied to Kamadeva, is its season. The ten verses take about six minutes to read. Here you will find the complete stotram in Devanagari, a Roman transliteration set beside it for those who do not read the script, the Hindi meaning (arth) spelled out in full, and a free PDF download to keep for offline recitation.

All a renunciate owns is the kaupina, the loincloth, and Shankaracharya turned that single possession into a celebration of the blessed life in five verses. The Kaupina Panchakam answers each verse with the refrain that the wearer of the loincloth is fortune itself, teaching that real wealth lies in spiritual realization rather than in things. Reading it stirs vairagya, dispassion, contentment with almost nothing, and a steady detachment from the body and from worldly craving. Mondays and Saturdays are its days, with Guru Purnima and Maha Shivaratri as its festivals. Five short verses make for about four minutes of recitation. The complete Panchakam is given here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline reading.

Bilvamangala Thakur, once a worldly man turned one of Krishna's greatest devotees, poured his love into the Krishna Karnamrutam, nectar for the ears. This selection brings together eight of its key verses, among them the famous Madhurashtakam that finds Krishna's sweetness pervading everything, and an iconic verse cataloguing his ornaments and his beauty. The point of reciting them is plain: love and devotion for Krishna deepen. Wednesdays and Fridays are the days for it; Janmashtami, Holi, and Radha Ashtami its festivals. The eight verses take about five minutes to read. On this page they appear in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each, and a free PDF download you can save and recite offline.

One hundred and eight names of Lakshmi, recited in sequence, form this Shatanamavali drawn from the Sri Suktam and Lakshmi Tantra. Each name is a facet of the Goddess of wealth, and reciting the full string is held to bestow prosperity, to lift poverty, debts, and financial difficulty for good, and to invoke the blessings of all eight forms, the Ashta Lakshmi. It belongs to Lakshmi Puja, to Deepawali, and to Friday worship. Friday is its weekly day; Diwali, Sharad Purnima, and the Vaibhav Lakshmi Vrat its festivals. With one hundred and eight names the recitation takes about fifteen minutes. The complete namavali is presented here in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration to carry the chant, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

The Vishnu Purana sets this eight-verse hymn to Lakshmi in the context of her inseparable bond with Vishnu, and reciting it is meant to draw her presence into the home. What follows from that presence is the substance of the prayer: wealth, health, long life, and progeny, the warding off of poverty and misfortune, and the lifting of all inauspiciousness as the divine grace of both Vishnu and Lakshmi settles on the house. Friday is its day, and Diwali its festival. Eight verses come to about seven minutes of recitation, an easy length to keep up. You can read the complete stotram here in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration beside it for pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth) given in full, and a free PDF download to keep for offline recitation.

Ramakrishna Kavi's Mahishasura Mardini Stotram is a torrent of sound, and this excerpt lifts five of its verses for those who want the hymn in a shorter form. The selection praises Durga, the slayer of the buffalo-demon, in all her forms, invoking her protection and auspiciousness; tradition also recites it to wash away sin and gather spiritual merit. Tuesdays and Fridays are the days for it, and Navratri, Durga Puja, and Dussehra its festivals, the nine-night season when the slaying of Mahishasura is remembered. Five verses make for about four minutes of recitation. The excerpt appears on this page in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration to follow the rolling meter, the Hindi meaning (arth) laid out, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Vishnu's first descent took the shape of a fish, Matsya, who carried the seeds of life through the deluge, and this eleven-verse stotram from the Matsya Purana praises that earliest avatar. Devotees recite it for the protection the fish-form gives, especially against floods, water-related dangers, and natural calamities, the very perils Matsya was born to meet. It is also held to destroy sins and grant divine protection, and to bestow the knowledge and wisdom that Matsya, who taught the Puranas, embodies. Thursday is its day, and Matsya Jayanti its festival. The reading takes about eight minutes. The complete stotram is given here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

A Chalisa takes its name from its forty chaupais, chalis being forty, and framing dohas round the Murugan Chalisa out to forty-three verse-units in all. This is the comprehensive prayer to Lord Kartikeya, the warrior son of Shiva worshipped as Murugan in the south, and from this traditional Chalisa devotees ask for the destruction of enemies and obstacles, for knowledge, strength, intelligence, and spiritual power, and for the lifting of diseases, sorrows, and every defect from their lives. Tuesday is its day; Skanda Shashti and Thaipusam its festivals. The recitation runs about eight minutes. On this page the full Chalisa appears in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Nine planets, one shield. The Navagraha Kavach from the Vishnu Dharmottara Purana sets all nine grahas as armor around the body, ten verses meant to fend off planetary afflictions and doshas at once rather than one planet at a time. Within that protection the kavach is held to bring freedom from every disease and to draw down wealth, grains, and prosperity, so that the student gains knowledge and the seeker gains both means and children. No single weekday or festival is fixed for it; it is recited as ongoing planetary protection. The ten verses take about five minutes. The complete kavach is presented here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning (arth) in full, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Five faces, five powers, all turned outward to guard the devotee. The Panchamukhi Hanuman Kavach from the Sudarshana Samhita invokes Hanuman in his five-headed form for protection from every direction, and each face carries its own work: the Narasimha face dissolves fear and grants courage, the Garuda face neutralizes poisons and black magic, the Varaha face heals disease and bodily ailments. Recited up to seven times a day, the kavach is said to yield progressively greater benefit. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, with Hanuman Jayanti as its festival. The twelve verses take about eight minutes. The full kavach appears here in Devanagari, a Roman transliteration set beside it, the Hindi meaning (arth) spelled out, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

When the rites owed to departed ancestors go unperformed, astrology speaks of Pitru Dosha, and this stotram of ten verses, drawn from traditional sources tied to the Garuda Purana and addressed to Lord Vishnu, is its powerful remedy. Recitation is meant to appease the ancestors and win their blessings, to clear the obstacles their unfinished rites leave behind, and to restore family harmony while loosening generational curses and recurring misfortune. Saturdays and Tuesdays are its days, and Pitru Paksha, the fortnight set aside for the ancestors, is its season. The reading takes about seven minutes. The complete stotram is given on this page in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Radha's own armor, the Radha Kavacham from the Brahmavaivarta Purana, sets her protection across the body and the directions through one hundred and eight of her names, woven into eight verses. The point of wearing such armor is twofold: complete spiritual protection on one hand, and on the other the awakening of prema, the divine love and devotion that binds a soul to Radha and Krishna together. Tradition also credits it with bestowing all the siddhis and a deeper spiritual fulfillment. Wednesdays and Fridays are its days, with Radha Ashtami and Holi as its festivals. The eight verses take about four minutes. The complete kavacham appears here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Gayatri mantras compress an entire prayer into a few measured lines, and the Rama Gayatri, presented here with its several variations, does so for Lord Rama. Rooted in the Vedic Gayatri tradition and counted across three verses, it is chanted as japa to call down the blessings and protection of Rama and to invoke the four purusharthas together, dharma, artha, kama, and moksha. Regular repetition is held to dissolve sins and to ground the reciter in righteousness and moral strength. Thursdays and Sundays are its days, and Ram Navami its festival. Allowing for the variations, a full reading runs about six minutes. On this page the mantra and its forms appear in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

The Chamakam, paired with the Rudram in the Krishna Yajurveda, is the portion where the worshipper asks, again and again, for what should be granted, and this excerpt of six verses gives a working taste of it for Lord Shiva. The selection invokes Shiva in all his forms, calling for his protection and auspiciousness; in the older manner it is also recited to wash away sin and gather spiritual merit. Monday is its day, and Maha Shivaratri its festival. The six verses take about five minutes to recite. On this page the excerpt is set out in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to carry the Vedic cadence, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each line, and a free PDF download you can keep for offline recitation.

Forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, chalis being forty, and the framing dohas bring the full Saraswati Chalisa to forty-three verse-units. This traditional hymn to the goddess of learning is what students and artists turn to before exams and performances: it asks for academic excellence and intellectual growth, for mastery over the arts, music, and literature, for the clearing of ignorance and mental dullness, and for eloquence and clarity of speech. Thursday is its day, and Saraswati Puja, when books and instruments are placed before her, is its festival. The recitation runs about twelve minutes. The complete Chalisa appears here in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Eleven verses, traditional in origin, gather the many gifts of Saraswati into a single hymn of praise. The Saraswati Stotram asks for mastery across every branch of knowledge, for poetic and literary talent, and for eloquence in place of any defect of speech; tradition even likens the wisdom it bestows to Kamadhenu, the wish-fulfilling cow. Thursday is the day for it, and Saraswati Puja its festival, the occasion when the goddess of knowledge is honored directly. The reading takes about eight minutes, an easy fit for a daily routine. On this page the complete stotram is given in Devanagari, with a Roman transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth) laid out in full, and a free PDF download to keep for offline recitation.

King Dasharatha, Rama's father, once composed a hymn to turn aside the wrath of Shani, and that hymn survives in the Skanda Purana's Shani Mahatmya as nine verses. It holds ten names of Saturn whose recitation is said to keep every Saturn affliction at bay, which makes it among the most powerful remedies for Sade Sati, for Dhaiya, and for the Shani Mahadasha. Tradition records that Dasharatha won from Shani the boon that the planet would never trouble his devotees; reciting it on Saturday morning is held to bring complete Shani shanti. Saturday is its day, with Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya as its festivals. The nine verses take about four minutes. The full text appears here in Devanagari with Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF for offline use.

This Ashtakam of eight verses comes out of the Advaita tradition and is offered to Lord Shiva, a hymn for devotion and steady spiritual growth. It invokes the blessings of Shankaracharya in all his forms and asks for protection and auspiciousness; in the older manner it is also recited to clear sin and earn spiritual merit. Two days carry it, Thursday and Monday, and two festivals mark it, Guru Purnima and Shankaracharya Jayanti, the latter remembering the great teacher of non-duality. The eight verses take about four minutes to recite. On this page the complete Ashtakam is set out in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration running alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download you can save and recite offline.

Adi Shankaracharya set this hymn to Shiva in the Bhujanga meter, the gliding, serpentine rhythm that gives the Shiva Bhujanga Stotram its name, and worked across fifteen verses. Devotees recite it to be freed from the cycle of birth and death and to win the spiritual knowledge and Vedantic realization the verses carry; tradition also turns to it to dissolve sin, to purify the mind, and to fulfill desires through Shiva's grace. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan as its seasons. The fifteen verses take about ten minutes to recite. The complete stotram appears here in Devanagari, a Roman transliteration set beside it to carry the meter, the Hindi meaning (arth) in full, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Known by the refrain Tasmai Namah that closes its verses, salutations to him, this Shiva Ashtakam from traditional Shaivite literature offers eight verses of homage to the Lord. Reciters seek through it Shiva's supreme blessings, the clearing of sins and worldly attachments, spiritual wisdom and inner peace, and protection against every calamity and evil force. Monday is its day, and Maha Shivaratri together with the Shravan Somvars, the Mondays of Shravan, are its festivals. The eight verses take about eight minutes to recite. On this page the complete Ashtakam is given in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to guide the chant, the Hindi meaning (arth) spelled out for each verse, and a free PDF download you can keep for offline recitation.

A dhun is meant to be sung in chorus until the room itself begins to vibrate, and the Shiva Tandava Dhun, a traditional devotional composition of five verses, invokes the Nataraja form of Shiva, the cosmic dancer. Singing it fills the surroundings with divine energy, shakes off lethargy, and rouses spiritual consciousness, which is why it is a favorite for Shiva worship gatherings. Monday is its day, and Maha Shivaratri along with the Shravan Somvars are its festivals. The five verses take about five minutes to sing through. On this page the full dhun is set out in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) laid out, and a free PDF download so you can carry it to a gathering or recite it offline.

When the personified Vedas sing the praises of the Supreme, the result is the Shruti Gita, set down in the Srimad Bhagavata Purana at chapter eighty-seven of the tenth canto. Its seven dense verses reveal how the Vedas themselves understand the Supreme Being and open deep philosophical insight into Brahman; reciting them is held to root out spiritual ignorance at its source and to lift consciousness to its highest plane, purifying the intellect for Vedantic realization. Thursday is its day, with Ekadashi and Guru Purnima as its festivals. The reading takes about eight minutes. On this page the complete gita is given in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline study.

A devotee setting out under the protection of Murugan recites the Skanda Sashti Kavacham, a beloved Tamil-Sanskrit hymn of twelve verses that builds a divine armor around the body. Each section calls on Skanda to guard a part of the person, and the whole is meant to dispel the fear of death, disease, and enemies, to prevent untimely death and cure illness, and to drive off every enemy and negative force. Tuesday is its day, and Kartik Purnima its festival. The twelve verses take about ten minutes to recite. On this page the complete kavacham appears in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to carry its driving rhythm, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download you can keep for offline recitation.

Chanted as the sun clears the horizon, the Surya Gayatri turns the first light of day into prayer. Gathered here are three Gayatri mantras from the Rig Veda addressed to Lord Surya, and reciting them at sunrise is held to charge the body with vitality, energy, and radiance, to ease eye diseases and skin ailments, and to strengthen the Sun's standing in one's horoscope. Sunday is the day for it; Makar Sankranti, Ratha Saptami, and Chhath Puja, the great solar festivals, are its seasons. Being only three short mantras, the recitation takes about two minutes, easy to fold into a morning routine. On this page all three appear in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth), and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

For anyone setting out on the path of Vedanta, the Tattva Bodha of Adi Shankaracharya is the usual first text, and this selection of fifteen key verses, offered here under Lord Shiva, lays its groundwork. It opens with the Sadhana Chatushtaya, the four qualifications a seeker needs, then clarifies the nature of the Atman, the three bodies, and the five sheaths, before turning to the meaning of Tat Tvam Asi and the other Mahavakyas. Mondays and Thursdays are its days, with Guru Purnima as its festival. The fifteen verses take about twelve minutes to work through. On this page they appear in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each, and a free PDF download for offline study.

Vaidyanath is Shiva the physician, and his shrine is the ninth of the twelve Jyotirlingas; reciting this ten-verse stotram from the Shiva Purana is held to equal a pilgrimage to that very lingam. Devotees turn to it for healing from diseases and ailments of every kind, for longer life and better health, and for the clearing of sins and negative karma that the healing form of Shiva is said to grant. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the Shravan Somvars as its festivals. The ten verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page the complete stotram is given in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration to guide pronunciation, the Hindi meaning (arth) in full, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Breath is life, and the Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram names the lord of breath twelve times, drawing its names from the Rig Veda across five verses to strengthen pranashakti, the vital energy. Tradition associates the recitation with relief from respiratory diseases and breathing disorders, with greater physical strength and vitality, and with long life given by the grace of Vayu Deva. Saturdays and Tuesdays are its days, and Hanuman Jayanti, honoring the son of Vayu, is its festival. Being only five short verses, the stotram takes about three minutes to recite, easy to keep up daily. On this page the twelve names appear in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each, and a free PDF download you can save for offline recitation.

Before a wedding, a new venture, or any ceremony, families recite a mangala stotram to set an auspicious tone, and this one from the Vishnu Purana does that work in eight verses. It invokes Vishnu's blessings for prosperity and protection, clears inauspiciousness and negative energy from the home, and settles peace and harmony over the household, which is why it suits the start of anything new. Three days carry it, Wednesday, Thursday, and Sunday, and three festivals mark it, Ekadashi, Diwali, and Vaikuntha Ekadashi. The eight verses take about five minutes to recite. On this page the complete stotram is set out in Devanagari with a Roman transliteration, the Hindi meaning (arth) for each verse, and a free PDF download you can keep for offline recitation.
